title: 9.3 Gram-Schmidt orthogonalization
description: Apply Gram-Schmidt to turn a basis into an orthogonal or orthonormal basis while preserving the same span.

body.raw:
An orthogonal basis is valuable, but most bases are not orthogonal when they are
first given to you. Gram-Schmidt is the standard procedure that repairs that
problem.

It starts from any linearly independent list and systematically removes the
components that point in already-used directions. What remains is orthogonal,
and the span is preserved at every step.

## The projection idea behind Gram-Schmidt

Suppose `v_1,\dots,v_k` are already orthogonal and `w` is any vector. If you
want the part of `w` that is perpendicular to every `v_i`, you subtract the
components of `w` along those vectors:

$$
w-
\frac{\langle w,v_1\rangle}{\|v_1\|^2}v_1
-\cdots-
\frac{\langle w,v_k\rangle}{\|v_k\|^2}v_k.
$$

<TheoremCard title="Orthogonal remainder theorem">
Let `S=\{v_1,\dots,v_k\}` be an orthogonal subset of `\mathbb{R}^m`, and let
`w\in\mathbb{R}^m`. Define

$$
v=
w-
\frac{\langle w,v_1\rangle}{\|v_1\|^2}v_1
-\cdots-
\frac{\langle w,v_k\rangle}{\|v_k\|^2}v_k.
$$

Then `v` is perpendicular to each `v_i`.
</TheoremCard>

This theorem is the engine of the algorithm. It tells you exactly how to build a
new vector orthogonal to all the old ones.

## Gram-Schmidt process

<TheoremCard title="Gram-Schmidt orthogonalization process">
Let `\{w_1,\dots,w_k\}` be a linearly independent subset of `\mathbb{R}^m`.
Define

$$
v_1=w_1,
$$

and for `\ell=2,\dots,k`,

$$
v_\ell=
w_\ell
-\frac{\langle w_\ell,v_1\rangle}{\|v_1\|^2}v_1
-\cdots-
\frac{\langle w_\ell,v_{\ell-1}\rangle}{\|v_{\ell-1}\|^2}v_{\ell-1}.
$$

Then:

1. `\{v_1,\dots,v_k\}` is orthogonal;
2. for each `\ell`,

$$
\operatorname{span}\{w_1,\dots,w_\ell\}
=
\operatorname{span}\{v_1,\dots,v_\ell\}.
$$

In particular, the final orthogonal set spans the same subspace as the original
linearly independent set.
</TheoremCard>

The second conclusion matters as much as the first. Gram-Schmidt does not merely
produce some orthogonal vectors. It preserves the exact subspace you started
with.

## How to read the algorithm

The first vector is unchanged:

$$
v_1=w_1.
$$

The second vector is the part of `w_2` orthogonal to `v_1`.

The third vector is the part of `w_3` orthogonal to both `v_1` and `v_2`.

And so on. Each step removes everything already accounted for by the earlier
orthogonal directions.

<WorkedExample title="A short Gram-Schmidt computation in R^3">
Start with the linearly independent vectors

$$
w_1=
\begin{bmatrix}
1\\0\\1
\end{bmatrix},
\qquad
w_2=
\begin{bmatrix}
1\\1\\1
\end{bmatrix},
\qquad
w_3=
\begin{bmatrix}
0\\1\\2
\end{bmatrix}.
$$

Set

$$
v_1=w_1=
\begin{bmatrix}
1\\0\\1
\end{bmatrix}.
$$

Now compute

$$
\frac{\langle w_2,v_1\rangle}{\|v_1\|^2}
=\frac{2}{2}=1,
$$

so

$$
v_2=w_2-v_1=
\begin{bmatrix}
0\\1\\0
\end{bmatrix}.
$$

Next,

$$
\frac{\langle w_3,v_1\rangle}{\|v_1\|^2}
=\frac{2}{2}=1,
\qquad
\frac{\langle w_3,v_2\rangle}{\|v_2\|^2}
=\frac{1}{1}=1.
$$

Therefore

$$
v_3=w_3-v_1-v_2=
\begin{bmatrix}
-1\\0\\1
\end{bmatrix}.
$$

The resulting set

$$
\left\{
\begin{bmatrix}
1\\0\\1
\end{bmatrix},
\begin{bmatrix}
0\\1\\0
\end{bmatrix},
\begin{bmatrix}
-1\\0\\1
\end{bmatrix}
\right\}
$$

is orthogonal and spans the same subspace as the original list.
</WorkedExample>

<WorkedExample title="A static Gram-Schmidt table">
The same calculation can be organized as a table, which is often clearer in a
written solution or exported study sheet.

Start with

$$
w_1=
\begin{bmatrix}1\\1\\0\end{bmatrix},
\quad
w_2=
\begin{bmatrix}1\\0\\1\end{bmatrix},
\quad
w_3=
\begin{bmatrix}0\\1\\1\end{bmatrix}.
$$

| Step | Projection data | New orthogonal vector | Span preserved |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| `v_1` | none | `v_1=w_1=(1,1,0)^T` | `Span\{v_1\}=Span\{w_1\}` |
| `v_2` | `\langle w_2,v_1\rangle/\|v_1\|^2=1/2` | `v_2=w_2-\frac12v_1=(1/2,-1/2,1)^T` | `Span\{v_1,v_2\}=Span\{w_1,w_2\}` |
| `v_3` | `\langle w_3,v_1\rangle/\|v_1\|^2=1/2`, `\langle w_3,v_2\rangle/\|v_2\|^2=1/3` | `v_3=w_3-\frac12v_1-\frac13v_2=(-2/3,2/3,2/3)^T` | `Span\{v_1,v_2,v_3\}=Span\{w_1,w_2,w_3\}` |

The table makes two things explicit. Each new vector is obtained by subtracting
projections onto the already constructed orthogonal vectors, and each step keeps
the same partial span.
</WorkedExample>

## From orthogonal to orthonormal

Gram-Schmidt gives an orthogonal basis. To get an orthonormal basis, normalize
each nonzero vector:

$$
u_i=\frac{v_i}{\|v_i\|}.
$$

<TheoremCard title="Every subspace has an orthonormal basis">
Let `V` be a subspace of `\mathbb{R}^m`. Then `V` has an orthogonal basis, and
after normalization it also has an orthonormal basis.
</TheoremCard>

So orthonormal bases are not rare or special accidents. Every finite-dimensional
subspace of `\mathbb{R}^m` admits one.

<WorkedExample title="Normalize the Gram-Schmidt output">
Using the vectors from the previous example:

$$
\|v_1\|=\sqrt2,\qquad
\|v_2\|=1,\qquad
\|v_3\|=\sqrt2.
$$

So an orthonormal basis is

$$
u_1=\frac{1}{\sqrt2}
\begin{bmatrix}
1\\0\\1
\end{bmatrix},
\qquad
u_2=
\begin{bmatrix}
0\\1\\0
\end{bmatrix},
\qquad
u_3=\frac{1}{\sqrt2}
\begin{bmatrix}
-1\\0\\1
\end{bmatrix}.
$$
</WorkedExample>

## Common mistake

<CommonMistake title="Do not subtract projections onto the original w-vectors">
In the recursive formula, each new vector must be built using the already
constructed orthogonal vectors `v_1,\dots,v_{\ell-1}`, not the original
`w_1,\dots,w_{\ell-1}`. If you subtract components along the original
non-orthogonal vectors, the output need not become orthogonal.
</CommonMistake>

## Quick check

<QuickCheck prompt="What is the first Gram-Schmidt vector v_1?">
Look at the definition.
</QuickCheck>

<RevealSolution title="Answer">
It is just `v_1=w_1`.
</RevealSolution>

<QuickCheck prompt="What property does Gram-Schmidt preserve besides orthogonality?">
Think about the span conclusion in the theorem.
</QuickCheck>

<RevealSolution title="Answer">
It preserves span. After each step, the new vectors span exactly the same
subspace as the original vectors up to that step.
</RevealSolution>

<QuickCheck prompt="How do you turn an orthogonal basis into an orthonormal basis?">
Use normalization.
</QuickCheck>

<RevealSolution title="Answer">
Divide each nonzero basis vector by its norm.
</RevealSolution>

## Exercises

<QuickCheck prompt="Apply the first two steps of Gram-Schmidt to `w_1=(1,1,0)` and `w_2=(1,0,1)`.">
Compute `v_1=w_1`, then subtract the component of `w_2` along `v_1`.
</QuickCheck>

<RevealSolution title="Guided solution">
First,

$$
v_1=
\begin{bmatrix}
1\\1\\0
\end{bmatrix}.
$$

Now

$$
\frac{\langle w_2,v_1\rangle}{\|v_1\|^2}
=\frac{1}{2}.
$$

So

$$
v_2=
\begin{bmatrix}
1\\0\\1
\end{bmatrix}
-\frac12
\begin{bmatrix}
1\\1\\0
\end{bmatrix}
=
\begin{bmatrix}
1/2\\-1/2\\1
\end{bmatrix}.
$$
</RevealSolution>

<QuickCheck prompt="Why does Gram-Schmidt require the original list `w_1,\dots,w_k` to be linearly independent?">
Think about what could happen to one of the new vectors.
</QuickCheck>

<RevealSolution title="Guided solution">
If the original list is dependent, one of the later vectors could lie entirely
in the span of the earlier ones. Then the orthogonal remainder would be `0`,
which cannot belong to an orthogonal basis.
</RevealSolution>

<QuickCheck prompt="Suppose Gram-Schmidt gives orthogonal vectors `v_1,v_2` with norms 3 and 4. What are the corresponding orthonormal vectors?">
Normalize each one separately.
</QuickCheck>

<RevealSolution title="Guided solution">
They are

$$
u_1=\frac{v_1}{3},
\qquad
u_2=\frac{v_2}{4}.
$$
</RevealSolution>

## Related notes

Read
[9.2 Orthogonal sets and orthonormal bases](/en/notes/math1030/inner-products/orthogonal-sets-and-orthonormal-bases)
first, because Gram-Schmidt is built from the orthogonal coefficient formula.

The existence theorem here is one of the main practical tools for working with
subspaces introduced earlier in
[6.2 Subspaces](/en/notes/math1030/vector-spaces/subspaces)
and [6.5 Basis and dimension](/en/notes/math1030/vector-spaces/basis-and-dimension).

The equality cases in the next note,
[9.4 Cauchy-Schwarz and triangle inequalities](/en/notes/math1030/inner-products/cauchy-schwarz-and-triangle-inequalities),
explain why the projection steps here behave geometrically.
